MUSA 6310: Assignment 3

Dennis Espejo

Part 1: Recreating a “Crush” Map

My “Crush” Map

I chose this map from the New York Times as my “crush” map. Beyond its aesthetic appeal -orange is my favorite color- I think this map successfully used circles to operationalize the raw out-migration data. I was initially confused about how a proportional symbol map displays data in ways a choropleth map can’t. After some reading and AI consultations, it’s become clearer how the proportional symbol map shows raw differences across areas that aren’t constrained by geographic boundaries. For something like “outmigration,” counts are especially useful to display data. The symbols are proportional to the raw count, not the population itself (which still often translates to a population number), which helps counties in smaller geographic areas with large populations, like New York, stand out from counties with large geographic areas and small populations, like those in Colorado.

The original cartographer used orange and green for their proportional shapes/circles, which are colors not on a gradient, indicating that the colors represent categorical data (the county had more immigration than emigration, and vice versa). Additionally, the cartographer only labeled areas where clusters of counties show a recorded pattern of large immigration/emigration (though I’m unsure why they labeled Albuquerque). The use of the soft orange/tan fill is a good use of color that neither takes away from the symbol’s colors nor obscures the border lines, while still being visible enough to identify these “high-risk” counties

I decided to recreate my crush map using Philadelphia data on residents with a bachelors degree or higher. I decided to focus on changes in the number of residents with a bachelor’s degree or higher because this variable relates to my field.

I first grabbed a shapefile of Philadelphia neighborhoods. When displaying data, neighborhoods have more meaning than census tracts, so I was determined to have this be a final map aspect.

Code 
neighborhoods= gpd.read_file("/Users/dennisespejo/Desktop/philadelphia-neighborhoods")

159 rows × 6 columns

Then I qued the census API to get me data of residents with Bachelors degree or higher. I grabbed the five-year ACS for both 2017 and 2021 so I can have a temporal aspect similar to my “crush” map where I can show which neighborhoods have had a decrease or increase of residents with a bachelors degree or higher between 2017 and 2021.

2022 Data:

Code 
import requests
import pandas as pd


api_key = 'e4257eb23558f9d666c87ad6dc303db358377420'


base_url = "https://api.census.gov/data/2022/acs/acs5"


params = {
    "get": "B03002_001E,B19013_001E,B15003_021E,B15003_022E,B15003_023E,B15003_024E,B15003_025E",
    "for": "tract:*",  
    "in": "state:42 county:101",  
    "key": api_key
}


response = requests.get(base_url, params=params)

if response.status_code != 200:
    print("Error: Failed to retrieve data")
    print(response.text)
else:
    print("Request successful")
    data = response.json()

  
    columns = data[0]  
    values = data[1:]  


    df = pd.DataFrame(values, columns=columns)
    
    
    df["college"] = (

    df["B15003_022E"].astype(int) +   # bachelors
    df["B15003_023E"].astype(int) +   # masters
    df["B15003_024E"].astype(int) +   # professional
    df["B15003_025E"].astype(int)     # doctorate
)


        
 


df.head()


Request successful

2017 data:

Code 
import requests
import pandas as pd


api_key = 'e4257eb23558f9d666c87ad6dc303db358377420'


base_url = "https://api.census.gov/data/2017/acs/acs5"


params = {
    "get": "B03002_001E,B19013_001E,B15003_021E,B15003_022E,B15003_023E,B15003_024E,B15003_025E",
    "for": "tract:*",  
    "in": "state:42 county:101",  
    "key": api_key
}


response = requests.get(base_url, params=params)

if response.status_code != 200:
    print("Error: Failed to retrieve data")
    print(response.text)
else:
    print("Request successful")
    data = response.json()

  
    columns = data[0]  
    values = data[1:]  


    df2 = pd.DataFrame(values, columns=columns)
    
    
    df2["college"] = (

    df2["B15003_022E"].astype(int) +   # bachelors
    df2["B15003_023E"].astype(int) +   # masters
    df2["B15003_024E"].astype(int) +   # professional
    df2["B15003_025E"].astype(int)     # doctorate
)


        
 


df2.head()

Code 
df["GEOID"] = df["state"] + df["county"] + df["tract"]
df2["GEOID"] = df2["state"] + df2["county"] + df2["tract"]

df_change = df.merge(
    df2,
    on="GEOID",
    suffixes=("_new", "_old")
)


df_change["college_change"] = (
    df_change["college_new"] - df_change["college_old"]
)

Grabbing Census Tract Shapefiles to Match with Census data, and changing to EPSG most suited for Philadelphia (32129):

import pygris

tracts = pygris.tracts(state = "PA", county = "Philadelphia")


tracts = tracts.merge(df_change, on="GEOID")


tracts=tracts.to_crs("EPSG:32129")


neighborhoods=neighborhoods.to_crs("EPSG:32129")
Using the default year of 2021
Using FIPS code '42' for input 'PA'
Using FIPS code '101' for input 'Philadelphia'

Joining with Census Tract Raw Data:

Merging Neighborhoods With Tracts:

Code 
tract_centroids = tracts.copy()
tract_centroids["geometry"] = tract_centroids.geometry.centroid

joined = gpd.sjoin(
    tract_centroids,
    neighborhoods,
    predicate="within",
    how="left"
)

Code 
neighborhood_change = joined.groupby("LISTNAME", as_index=False)["college_change"].sum()

neighborhoods = neighborhoods.merge(neighborhood_change, on="LISTNAME", how="left")
neighborhoods["college_change"] = neighborhoods["college_change"].fillna(0)

centroids = neighborhoods.copy()
centroids["geometry"] = neighborhoods.geometry.representative_point()

centroids["direction"] = centroids["college_change"].apply(
    lambda x: "increase" if x > 0 else ("decrease" if x < 0 else "no change")
)

centroids["size"] = centroids["college_change"].abs() ** 0.5 * 10

centroids = centroids[centroids["direction"] != "no change"].copy()
neighborhoods

I also added a shapefile from Open Data Philly of areas in Philadelphia with a college nearby. I did this to make my “shaded” areas on my map similar to my crush map’s shaded “high-risk” areas.

Code 
colleges=gpd.read_file("/Users/dennisespejo/Desktop/Universities_Colleges")


colleges = gpd.read_file("/Users/dennisespejo/Desktop/Universities_Colleges")

colleges = colleges.to_crs(neighborhoods.crs)


college_join = gpd.sjoin(
    colleges,
    neighborhoods,
    predicate="within",
    how="left"
)


college_flag = college_join.groupby("LISTNAME").size().reset_index(name="college_count")
college_flag["has_college"] = True

neighborhoods = neighborhoods.merge(college_flag[["LISTNAME", "has_college"]],
                                    on="LISTNAME",
                                    how="left")

neighborhoods["has_college"] = neighborhoods["has_college"].fillna(False)
/var/folders/m5/3nc74hbn0zj7sm8pv25ds7340000gn/T/ipykernel_16568/2250460843.py:26: FutureWarning: Downcasting object dtype arrays on .fillna, .ffill, .bfill is deprecated and will change in a future version. Call result.infer_objects(copy=False) instead. To opt-in to the future behavior, set `pd.set_option('future.no_silent_downcasting', True)`
  neighborhoods["has_college"] = neighborhoods["has_college"].fillna(False)

“Crush” Map Recreation

Code 
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import matplotlib as mpl
import matplotlib.patheffects as pe
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd

# ============================================================
# NYT-style Philadelphia neighborhood bubble map
# labels moved outside map with connector lines
# ============================================================

mpl.rcParams["hatch.linewidth"] = 0.5
mpl.rcParams["font.family"] = "DejaVu Sans"

# ----------------------------
# colors / styling
# ----------------------------
base_fill     = "#f7f6f3"
base_edge     = "#d6d0c8"
outer_edge    = "#bfb8af"

college_fill  = "#f3ece4"
college_hatch = "#ddc3a7"

inc_color     = "#71d2cb"
dec_color     = "#ef8f78"

title_color   = "#111111"
text_color    = "#4d4d4d"
subtle_text   = "#7f7f7f"
label_color   = "#5f5f5f"
leader_color  = "#a7a09a"

# ----------------------------
# prepare plotting data
# ----------------------------
plot_points = centroids[centroids["direction"] != "no change"].copy()

name_candidates = ["neighborhood", "NAME", "name", "LISTNAME", "MAPNAME"]
name_col = next((c for c in name_candidates if c in plot_points.columns), None)

if name_col is None:
    raise ValueError("Could not find a neighborhood name column in centroids.")

if neighborhoods.crs != plot_points.crs:
    plot_points = plot_points.to_crs(neighborhoods.crs)

# ----------------------------
# bubble scaling
# ----------------------------
abs_change = plot_points["college_change"].abs()

vmin = abs_change.min()
vmax = abs_change.max()

min_size = 18
max_size = 900
size_exp = 0.72

if vmax == vmin:
    plot_points["plot_size"] = (min_size + max_size) / 2
else:
    scaled = ((abs_change - vmin) / (vmax - vmin)) ** size_exp
    plot_points["plot_size"] = min_size + scaled * (max_size - min_size)

inc = plot_points[plot_points["direction"] == "increase"].copy()
dec = plot_points[plot_points["direction"] == "decrease"].copy()

# ----------------------------
# biggest increase / decrease
# ----------------------------
max_row = plot_points.loc[plot_points["college_change"].idxmax()]
min_row = plot_points.loc[plot_points["college_change"].idxmin()]

max_name = str(max_row[name_col]).title()
min_name = str(min_row[name_col]).title()
max_val = int(round(max_row["college_change"]))
min_val = int(round(min_row["college_change"]))

# ----------------------------
# choose labels
# ----------------------------
top_inc_labels = plot_points.nlargest(5, "college_change").copy()
top_dec_labels = plot_points.nsmallest(5, "college_change").copy()
label_df = pd.concat([top_inc_labels, top_dec_labels], axis=0).drop_duplicates(subset=[name_col]).copy()

anchor_names = [
    "Center City", "University City", "West Philadelphia", "North Philadelphia",
    "South Philadelphia", "Kensington", "Roxborough", "Chestnut Hill",
    "Fishtown", "Germantown"
]

if name_col in neighborhoods.columns:
    anchor_rows = neighborhoods[neighborhoods[name_col].isin(anchor_names)].copy()
else:
    anchor_rows = pd.DataFrame()

if len(anchor_rows) > 0:
    anchor_centroids = anchor_rows.copy()
    anchor_centroids["geometry"] = anchor_centroids.geometry.representative_point()
    for col in plot_points.columns:
        if col not in anchor_centroids.columns:
            anchor_centroids[col] = np.nan
    label_df = pd.concat(
        [label_df, anchor_centroids[label_df.columns]],
        axis=0
    ).drop_duplicates(subset=[name_col])

label_df = label_df.copy()
label_df["label_geom"] = label_df.geometry.representative_point()

# ----------------------------
# figure
# ----------------------------
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(17, 11.2))
fig.patch.set_facecolor("white")
ax.set_facecolor("white")

# ----------------------------
# base geography
# ----------------------------
neighborhoods.plot(
    ax=ax,
    color=base_fill,
    edgecolor="none",
    linewidth=0,
    zorder=1
)

neighborhoods[neighborhoods["has_college"]].plot(
    ax=ax,
    color=college_fill,
    edgecolor="none",
    linewidth=0,
    zorder=2
)

neighborhoods[neighborhoods["has_college"]].plot(
    ax=ax,
    facecolor="none",
    edgecolor=college_hatch,
    hatch="////",
    linewidth=0,
    zorder=2.4
)

neighborhoods.boundary.plot(
    ax=ax,
    color="#fbfaf8",
    linewidth=1.3,
    zorder=2.8
)

neighborhoods.boundary.plot(
    ax=ax,
    color=base_edge,
    linewidth=0.72,
    zorder=3
)

city_outline = neighborhoods.unary_union.boundary
try:
    import geopandas as gpd
    gpd.GeoSeries([city_outline], crs=neighborhoods.crs).plot(
        ax=ax,
        color=outer_edge,
        linewidth=1.2,
        zorder=3.2
    )
except Exception:
    neighborhoods.boundary.plot(
        ax=ax,
        color=outer_edge,
        linewidth=1.0,
        zorder=3.2
    )

# ----------------------------
# bubbles
# ----------------------------
dec.plot(
    ax=ax,
    markersize=dec["plot_size"],
    color=dec_color,
    alpha=0.74,
    edgecolor="white",
    linewidth=0.8,
    zorder=4
)

inc.plot(
    ax=ax,
    markersize=inc["plot_size"],
    color=inc_color,
    alpha=0.74,
    edgecolor="white",
    linewidth=0.8,
    zorder=5
)

# ----------------------------
# extent with extra room for outside labels
# ----------------------------
xmin, ymin, xmax, ymax = neighborhoods.total_bounds
xrange_ = xmax - xmin
yrange_ = ymax - ymin

left_label_x  = xmin - 0.18 * xrange_
right_label_x = xmax + 0.18 * xrange_

ax.set_xlim(xmin - 0.24 * xrange_, xmax + 0.24 * xrange_)
ax.set_ylim(ymin - 0.14 * yrange_, ymax + 0.14 * yrange_)

# ----------------------------
# external label placement
# split labels into left/right columns
# ----------------------------
label_df["x"] = label_df["label_geom"].x
label_df["y"] = label_df["label_geom"].y

mid_x = (xmin + xmax) / 2
left_df = label_df[label_df["x"] < mid_x].sort_values("y", ascending=False).copy()
right_df = label_df[label_df["x"] >= mid_x].sort_values("y", ascending=False).copy()

def spaced_positions(y_values, top, bottom, min_gap):
    y_sorted = list(y_values)
    adjusted = []
    prev = None

    for y in y_sorted:
        yy = min(y, top)
        if prev is not None and yy > prev - min_gap:
            yy = prev - min_gap
        adjusted.append(yy)
        prev = yy

    if len(adjusted) > 0 and adjusted[-1] < bottom:
        shift = bottom - adjusted[-1]
        adjusted = [y + shift for y in adjusted]

        for i in range(1, len(adjusted)):
            if adjusted[i] > adjusted[i - 1] - min_gap:
                adjusted[i] = adjusted[i - 1] - min_gap

    return adjusted

top_y = ymax + 0.05 * yrange_
bottom_y = ymin - 0.05 * yrange_
min_gap = 0.06 * yrange_

left_targets = spaced_positions(left_df["y"].tolist(), top_y, bottom_y, min_gap)
right_targets = spaced_positions(right_df["y"].tolist(), top_y, bottom_y, min_gap)

left_df["y_target"] = left_targets
right_df["y_target"] = right_targets

# ----------------------------
# draw connector labels
# ----------------------------
def draw_side_labels(df_side, x_text, ha):
    for _, row in df_side.iterrows():
        x0 = row["x"]
        y0 = row["y"]
        y1 = row["y_target"]

        label_text = str(row[name_col]).title()
        is_logan = label_text.lower() == "logan"

        ax.annotate(
            label_text,
            xy=(x0, y0),
            xytext=(x_text, y1),
            textcoords="data",
            ha=ha,
            va="center",
            fontsize=20 if is_logan else 10.5,
            color=dec_color if is_logan else label_color,
            fontweight="bold" if is_logan else "normal",
            zorder=6,
            path_effects=[
                pe.Stroke(linewidth=3.2 if is_logan else 3.0, foreground="white", alpha=0.96),
                pe.Normal()
            ],
            arrowprops=dict(
                arrowstyle="-",
                color=leader_color,
                lw=1.2 if is_logan else 0.9,
                shrinkA=0,
                shrinkB=4,
                connectionstyle="angle3,angleA=0,angleB=90"
            )
        )

draw_side_labels(left_df, left_label_x, "right")
draw_side_labels(right_df, right_label_x, "left")

# ----------------------------
# title + subtitle
# ----------------------------
title_y = 0.952
line_gap = 0.035
x0 = 0.07

# ---- LINE 1 ----
fig.text(
    x0, title_y + .0008,
    "Logan",
    fontsize=24,
    family="serif",
    color=dec_color,  # orange
    fontweight="bold",
    ha="left",
    va="top"
)

fig.text(
    x0 + 0.069, title_y,
    " is the Philadelphia neighborhood with the ",
    fontsize=24,
    family="serif",
    color=title_color,
    ha="left",
    va="top"
)

fig.text(
    .069 + 0.499, title_y + .0008,
    "largest decrease",
    fontsize=24,
    family="serif",
    color=dec_color,  # orange
    fontweight="bold",
    ha="left",
    va="top"
)

# ---- LINE 2 ----
fig.text(
    x0, title_y - line_gap,
    "in raw counts of persons with a Bachelor Degree or Higher from 2017 to 2022",
    fontsize=24,
    family="serif",
    color=title_color,
    ha="left",
    va="top"
)

fig.text(
    0.07, 0.908,
    "",
    fontsize=10.5,
    color="#666666",
    ha="left",
    va="top"
)

# ----------------------------
# inline legend
# ----------------------------
legend_y = 0.842
swatch_x = 0.07
swatch_w = 0.018
swatch_h = 0.018

fig.patches.append(
    plt.Rectangle(
        (swatch_x, legend_y - swatch_h / 2),
        swatch_w,
        swatch_h,
        transform=fig.transFigure,
        facecolor=college_fill,
        edgecolor=college_hatch,
        linewidth=0.6,
        hatch="////",
        zorder=20
    )
)

fig.text(
    swatch_x + swatch_w + 0.008, legend_y,
    "College or university",
    fontsize=10,
    color=text_color,
    va="center",
    ha="left"
)

fig.text(0.215, legend_y, "|", fontsize=11, color="#9a9a9a", va="center")

fig.patches.append(
    plt.Circle(
        (0.24, legend_y), 0.0075,
        transform=fig.transFigure,
        facecolor=inc_color,
        edgecolor="white",
        linewidth=0.6,
        alpha=0.95
    )
)
fig.text(0.253, legend_y, "More degree holders", fontsize=10, color=text_color, va="center", ha="left")

fig.text(0.36, legend_y, "|", fontsize=11, color="#9a9a9a", va="center")

fig.patches.append(
    plt.Circle(
        (0.385, legend_y), 0.0075,
        transform=fig.transFigure,
        facecolor=dec_color,
        edgecolor="white",
        linewidth=0.6,
        alpha=0.95
    )
)
fig.text(0.398, legend_y, "Fewer degree holders", fontsize=10, color=text_color, va="center", ha="left")

# ----------------------------
# size legend
# ----------------------------
size_vals = [250, 750, 1500]

if vmax == vmin:
    size_markers = [500, 500, 500]
else:
    size_markers = []
    for v in size_vals:
        sv = ((v - vmin) / (vmax - vmin))
        sv = max(0, min(1, sv))
        sv = sv ** size_exp
        size_markers.append(min_size + sv * (max_size - min_size))

ax_leg = fig.add_axes([0.74, 0.76, 0.21, 0.11])
ax_leg.axis("off")
ax_leg.set_xlim(0, 1)
ax_leg.set_ylim(0, 1)

ax_leg.text(
    0.0, 0.98,
    "Absolute change in residents\nwith degrees",
    fontsize=10,
    color=text_color,
    va="top"
)

x_positions = [0.22, 0.53, 0.86]
y_circle = 0.36
y_label = 0.53

for x, s, label in zip(x_positions, size_markers, size_vals):
    ax_leg.scatter(
        x, y_circle,
        s=s,
        facecolor="none",
        edgecolor="#a6a6a6",
        linewidth=0.9
    )
    ax_leg.text(
        x, y_label,
        f"{label:,}",
        ha="center",
        va="bottom",
        fontsize=9,
        color=subtle_text
    )

# ----------------------------
# top movers note
# ----------------------------
base_x = 0.07
y_pos = 0.075

fig.text(
    base_x, y_pos,
    "Largest increase: ",
    fontsize=9.5,
    color="#666666",
    ha="left"
)

fig.text(
    base_x + 0.115, y_pos,
    f"{max_name} ({max_val:+,})",
    fontsize=9.5,
    color="#666666",
    ha="left"
)

fig.text(
    base_x + 0.32, y_pos,
    " | ",
    fontsize=9.5,
    color="#666666",
    ha="left"
)

fig.text(
    base_x + 0.34, y_pos,
    "Largest decrease: ",
    fontsize=9.5,
    color=dec_color,
    ha="left"
)

fig.text(
    base_x + 0.48, y_pos,
    f"{min_name} ({min_val:+,})",
    fontsize=9.5,
    color=dec_color,
    ha="left",
    fontweight="bold"
)

# ----------------------------
# source
# ----------------------------
fig.text(
    0.07, 0.05,
    "Source: American Community Survey 5-year estimates; Philadelphia neighborhood boundaries; college locations.",
    fontsize=8.5,
    color="#8a8a8a",
    ha="left"
)

ax.set_axis_off()
plt.subplots_adjust(left=0.02, right=0.98, top=0.86, bottom=0.08)
plt.show()

My map shows the change in residents with a bachelor’s degree or higher between 2017 and 2022. I replicated the color scheme from my crush map and used a similar tone for the shaded areas, which indicate neighborhoods that contain a college campus. Since the data are raw counts of people with a bachelor’s degree or higher, a proportional symbol map is appropriate for showing variation across space.

From class, one key takeaway was projection choice. For Philadelphia-based maps, EPSG:32129 is the most appropriate, so I used that here. My earlier crush map did not use this projection, as AI suggested it was likely built in EPSG:3857. Another class takeaway was the importance of highlighting a clear main finding directly on the map, using color to guide interpretation.

The most notable pattern is that Logan, which contains La Salle University, experienced the largest decrease in residents with a bachelor’s degree or higher over this period. This stands out given its proximity to a higher education institution and suggests a direction for future research on what is happening in that neighborhood.

What was hardest to replicate was how “clean” the original map looks, regarding how vibrant the colors look in comparison to mine which are much more diluted. Additionally, becasue my “crush” map is of the entire U.S., its easier to fit labels on the map. For my map, I had to use lines from each proportinal symbol that connects to the neighrbohood name that symbol is in outside of the map.

Part 2: Different Projections

Well-suited Projection (EPSG: 2163)

Code 
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import matplotlib.patheffects as pe
import numpy as np
from shapely.affinity import translate


cities_gdf = gpd.GeoDataFrame(
    cities,
    geometry=gpd.points_from_xy(cities["lon"], cities["lat"]),
    crs="EPSG:4326"
).to_crs(epsg=3857)


plot_points = centroids.copy()


us_states = us_states.to_crs(epsg=2163)
merged_gdf = merged_gdf.to_crs(epsg=2163)
plot_points = plot_points.to_crs(epsg=2163)
cities_gdf = cities_gdf.to_crs(epsg=2163)

# -----------------------------
# Reduce clustering before plotting
# -----------------------------

threshold = plot_points["abs_change"].quantile(0.35)
plot_points = plot_points[plot_points["abs_change"] > threshold].copy()





# Cap extreme values so a few very large counties do not dominate
cap = plot_points["abs_change"].quantile(0.90)
plot_points["abs_change_capped"] = np.minimum(plot_points["abs_change"], cap)

# Compress symbol sizes
plot_points["symbol_size"] = np.sqrt(plot_points["abs_change_capped"]) 

# Add slight jitter so centroids do not stack directly on top of each other
np.random.seed(42)
jitter_strength = 8000

x_jitter = np.random.uniform(-jitter_strength, jitter_strength, len(plot_points))
y_jitter = np.random.uniform(-jitter_strength, jitter_strength, len(plot_points))

plot_points["geometry"] = [
    translate(geom, xoff=dx, yoff=dy)
    for geom, dx, dy in zip(plot_points.geometry, x_jitter, y_jitter)
]

# Optional: remove the tiniest changes to reduce visual noise
threshold = plot_points["abs_change"].quantile(0.10)
plot_points = plot_points[plot_points["abs_change"] > threshold].copy()

# Re-split after jitter + filtering
increase_points_plot = plot_points[
    plot_points["change_bachelors_or_higher"] > 0
].copy()

decrease_points_plot = plot_points[
    plot_points["change_bachelors_or_higher"] < 0
].copy()

# -----------------------------
# Plot
# -----------------------------
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(18, 9), facecolor="white")
ax.set_facecolor("white")

# Manually place the map lower on the page
ax.set_position([0.04, 0.06, 0.92, 0.76])

# -----------------------------
# Base map styling
# -----------------------------
us_states.plot(
    ax=ax,
    color="#faf9f7",
    edgecolor="none",
    zorder=1
)

us_states.boundary.plot(
    ax=ax,
    color="#c8c8c3",
    linewidth=0.8,
    zorder=2
)

merged_gdf.boundary.plot(
    ax=ax,
    color="#e8e6e1",
    linewidth=0.08,
    alpha=0.25,
    zorder=2
)

# -----------------------------
# Points
# -----------------------------
if len(decrease_points_plot) > 0:
    decrease_points_plot.plot(
        ax=ax,
        markersize=decrease_points_plot["symbol_size"],
        color="#ef8a62",
        alpha=0.45,
        linewidth=0,
        zorder=3
    )

if len(increase_points_plot) > 0:
    increase_points_plot.plot(
        ax=ax,
        markersize=increase_points_plot["symbol_size"],
        color="#6fcfcb",
        alpha=0.45,
        linewidth=0,
        zorder=4
    )

# -----------------------------
# City labels
# -----------------------------
for _, row in cities_gdf.iterrows():
    txt = ax.text(
        row.geometry.x,
        row.geometry.y,
        row["city"],
        fontsize=row["fontsize"],
        color="#7b7b76",
        ha="center",
        va="center",
        family="DejaVu Sans",
        zorder=5
    )
    txt.set_path_effects([
        pe.Stroke(linewidth=2.8, foreground="white"),
        pe.Normal()
    ])

# -----------------------------
# Figure-level title
# -----------------------------
fig.text(
    0.5, 0.965,
    "The largest increases in bachelor’s degree or higher attainment\n"
"are concentrated in major metro areas across the Northeast, South, and West.",
    ha="center",
    va="top",
    fontsize=20,
    color="#3f3f3a",
    family="DejaVu Serif",
    linespacing=1.35
)

fig.text(
    0.04, 0.02,
    "Source: U.S. Census Bureau, American Community Survey (2017–2022)",
    ha="left",
    va="bottom",
    fontsize=9,
    color="#6a6a64",
    family="DejaVu Sans",
    bbox=dict(
        boxstyle="round,pad=0.3",
        facecolor="#f7f6f3",
        edgecolor="none"
    )
)

# -----------------------------
# Direction chips
# -----------------------------

fig.text(
    0.395, 0.85,
    "Increase in",
    ha="left",
    va="center",
    fontsize=10,
    color="#3f3f3a",
    family="DejaVu Sans",
    bbox=dict(
        boxstyle="round,pad=0.18",
        facecolor="#8ed9d5",
        edgecolor="none",
        alpha=0.8
    )
)

fig.text(
    0.445, 0.85,
    "or",
    ha="left",
    va="center",
    fontsize=10,
    color="#6a6a64",
    family="DejaVu Sans"
)

fig.text(
    0.46, 0.85,
    "Decrease in",
    ha="left",
    va="center",
    fontsize=10,
    color="#3f3f3a",
    family="DejaVu Sans",
    bbox=dict(
        boxstyle="round,pad=0.18",
        facecolor="#f2aa8f",
        edgecolor="none",
        alpha=0.85
    )
)

fig.text(
    0.51, 0.85,
    "residents with a bachelors degree or higher",
    ha="left",
    va="center",
    fontsize=10,
    color="#6a6a64",
    family="DejaVu Sans"
)


# -----------------------------
# Right-side legend header
# -----------------------------
fig.text(
    0.73, 0.88,
    "County net change in\nbachelor’s degree or higher, 2017–2022",
    ha="left",
    va="top",
    fontsize=11,
    color="#5c5c57",
    family="DejaVu Sans",
    weight="bold"
)

# -----------------------------
# Bubble size guide
# -----------------------------
guide_values = [5000, 50000]
guide_sizes = [np.sqrt(v) for v in guide_values]

guide_x = [0.765, 0.80]
guide_y = 0.80

for x, s in zip(guide_x, guide_sizes):
    ax.scatter(
        [x], [guide_y],
        s=s,
        facecolors="none",
        edgecolors="#b4b4ae",
        linewidths=1.2,
        transform=fig.transFigure,
        zorder=10
    )

fig.text(
    0.765, 0.82, "5k",
    ha="center",
    va="bottom",
    fontsize=12,
    color="#8b8b85",
    family="DejaVu Sans"
)

fig.text(
    0.80, 0.82, "50k",
    ha="center",
    va="bottom",
    fontsize=12,
    color="#8b8b85",
    family="DejaVu Sans"
)

# -----------------------------
# Clean up
# -----------------------------
ax.set_axis_off()
plt.show()

For my different projections map, I essentially recreated my “crush” map once again. This time, I am using data on residents who have attained a bachelor’s degree or higher across the entire United States. Based on our class discussion, the most appropriate projection for displaying national U.S. data is the USA Contiguous Lambert Azimuthal Equal-Area projection (EPSG:2163), as it minimizes distortion in area. This is especially important for my map because I am using proportional symbols, where maintaining accurate relative sizes across space ensures that comparisons between regions are not visually misleading.

Ill-fitting Projection (EPSG: 3035 )

Code 
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import matplotlib.patheffects as pe
import numpy as np
from shapely.affinity import translate

plot_points = centroids.copy()

# -----------------------------
# Reproject to an UNSUITED CRS for a U.S. national map
# EPSG:3857 = Web Mercator
# -----------------------------
us_states_bad = us_states.to_crs(epsg=3035)
merged_gdf_bad = merged_gdf.to_crs(epsg=3035)
plot_points_bad = plot_points.to_crs(epsg=3035)
cities_gdf_bad = cities_gdf.to_crs(epsg=3035)

# -----------------------------
# Reduce clustering before plotting
# -----------------------------
threshold = plot_points_bad["abs_change"].quantile(0.35)
plot_points_bad = plot_points_bad[
    plot_points_bad["abs_change"] > threshold
].copy()

# Cap extreme values so a few very large counties do not dominate
cap = plot_points_bad["abs_change"].quantile(0.90)
plot_points_bad["abs_change_capped"] = np.minimum(
    plot_points_bad["abs_change"],
    cap
)

# Compress symbol sizes
plot_points_bad["symbol_size"] = np.sqrt(
    plot_points_bad["abs_change_capped"]
) / 2

# Add slight jitter so centroids do not stack directly on top of each other
np.random.seed(42)
jitter_strength = 8000

x_jitter = np.random.uniform(-jitter_strength, jitter_strength, len(plot_points_bad))
y_jitter = np.random.uniform(-jitter_strength, jitter_strength, len(plot_points_bad))

plot_points_bad["geometry"] = [
    translate(geom, xoff=dx, yoff=dy)
    for geom, dx, dy in zip(plot_points_bad.geometry, x_jitter, y_jitter)
]

# Optional: remove the tiniest changes to reduce visual noise
threshold = plot_points_bad["abs_change"].quantile(0.10)
plot_points_bad = plot_points_bad[
    plot_points_bad["abs_change"] > threshold
].copy()

# Re-split after jitter + filtering
increase_points_plot = plot_points_bad[
    plot_points_bad["change_bachelors_or_higher"] > 0
].copy()

decrease_points_plot = plot_points_bad[
    plot_points_bad["change_bachelors_or_higher"] < 0
].copy()

# -----------------------------
# Plot
# -----------------------------
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(18, 9), facecolor="white")
ax.set_facecolor("white")

# Manually place the map lower on the page
ax.set_position([0.04, 0.06, 0.92, 0.76])

# -----------------------------
# Base map styling
# -----------------------------
us_states_bad.plot(
    ax=ax,
    color="#faf9f7",
    edgecolor="none",
    zorder=1
)

us_states_bad.boundary.plot(
    ax=ax,
    color="#c8c8c3",
    linewidth=0.8,
    zorder=2
)

merged_gdf_bad.boundary.plot(
    ax=ax,
    color="#e8e6e1",
    linewidth=0.08,
    alpha=0.25,
    zorder=2
)

# -----------------------------
# Points
# -----------------------------
if len(decrease_points_plot) > 0:
    decrease_points_plot.plot(
        ax=ax,
        markersize=decrease_points_plot["symbol_size"],
        color="#ef8a62",
        alpha=0.45,
        linewidth=0,
        zorder=3
    )

if len(increase_points_plot) > 0:
    increase_points_plot.plot(
        ax=ax,
        markersize=increase_points_plot["symbol_size"],
        color="#6fcfcb",
        alpha=0.45,
        linewidth=0,
        zorder=4
    )

# -----------------------------
# City labels
# -----------------------------
for _, row in cities_gdf_bad.iterrows():
    txt = ax.text(
        row.geometry.x,
        row.geometry.y,
        row["city"],
        fontsize=row["fontsize"],
        color="#7b7b76",
        ha="center",
        va="center",
        family="DejaVu Sans",
        zorder=5
    )
    txt.set_path_effects([
        pe.Stroke(linewidth=2.8, foreground="white"),
        pe.Normal()
    ])

# -----------------------------
# Figure-level title
# -----------------------------
fig.text(
    0.5, 0.965,
    "The largest increases in bachelor’s degree or higher attainment\n"
    "are concentrated in major metro areas across the Northeast, South, and West.",
    ha="center",
    va="top",
    fontsize=20,
    color="#3f3f3a",
    family="DejaVu Serif",
    linespacing=1.35
)

fig.text(
    0.04, 0.02,
    "Source: U.S. Census Bureau, American Community Survey (2017–2022)",
    ha="left",
    va="bottom",
    fontsize=9,
    color="#6a6a64",
    family="DejaVu Sans",
    bbox=dict(
        boxstyle="round,pad=0.3",
        facecolor="#f7f6f3",
        edgecolor="none"
    )
)

# -----------------------------
# Direction chips
# -----------------------------
fig.text(
    0.395, 0.85,
    "Increase in",
    ha="left",
    va="center",
    fontsize=10,
    color="#3f3f3a",
    family="DejaVu Sans",
    bbox=dict(
        boxstyle="round,pad=0.18",
        facecolor="#8ed9d5",
        edgecolor="none",
        alpha=0.8
    )
)

fig.text(
    0.445, 0.85,
    "or",
    ha="left",
    va="center",
    fontsize=10,
    color="#6a6a64",
    family="DejaVu Sans"
)

fig.text(
    0.46, 0.85,
    "Decrease in",
    ha="left",
    va="center",
    fontsize=10,
    color="#3f3f3a",
    family="DejaVu Sans",
    bbox=dict(
        boxstyle="round,pad=0.18",
        facecolor="#f2aa8f",
        edgecolor="none",
        alpha=0.85
    )
)

fig.text(
    0.51, 0.85,
    "residents with a bachelors degree or higher",
    ha="left",
    va="center",
    fontsize=10,
    color="#6a6a64",
    family="DejaVu Sans"
)

# -----------------------------
# Right-side legend header
# -----------------------------
fig.text(
    0.73, 0.88,
    "County net change in\nbachelor’s degree or higher, 2017–2022",
    ha="left",
    va="top",
    fontsize=11,
    color="#5c5c57",
    family="DejaVu Sans",
    weight="bold"
)

# -----------------------------
# Bubble size guide
# -----------------------------
guide_values = [5000, 50000]
guide_sizes = [np.sqrt(v) for v in guide_values]

guide_x = [0.765, 0.80]
guide_y = 0.80

for x, s in zip(guide_x, guide_sizes):
    ax.scatter(
        [x], [guide_y],
        s=s,
        facecolors="none",
        edgecolors="#b4b4ae",
        linewidths=1.2,
        transform=fig.transFigure,
        zorder=10
    )

fig.text(
    0.765, 0.82, "5k",
    ha="center",
    va="bottom",
    fontsize=12,
    color="#8b8b85",
    family="DejaVu Sans"
)

fig.text(
    0.80, 0.82, "50k",
    ha="center",
    va="bottom",
    fontsize=12,
    color="#8b8b85",
    family="DejaVu Sans"
)

# -----------------------------
# Clean up
# -----------------------------
ax.set_axis_off()
plt.show()

For my ill-fitting projection map, I recreated the same visualization using the ETRS89 / LAEA Europe projection (EPSG:3035). This projection is designed as a Lambert Azimuthal Equal Area projection centered on Europe. However, in my map of the United States, it does not preserve area in any meaningful way. Visually, the U.S. appears rotated, tilted, and spatially compressed, and it no longer resembles its recognizable geographic form. This distorts appearance and how we interpret distance and regional relationships across the map.

For my proportional symbol map, this becomes especially problematic. The data itself has not changed, but the projection distorts the space the data sits on, making the placement and distribution of symbols misleading. Instead of helping reveal patterns, the projection introduces confusion. In short, EPSG:3035 works well for Europe because it preserves area and minimizes distortion there, but when applied to the U.S., it fails to maintain those properties and undermines the interpretability of the map.

MAP 3: Retouching an Old Map

For my third map, I’ll be retouching one of the maps from my final project in Geospatial Dara Science in Python (MUSA 5500) Course I took in the Fall (2024) semester.

Code 
earth_tone_palette = [
"#ffffe5",
"#fff7bc",
"#fee391",
"#fec44f",
"#fe9929",
"#ec7014",
"#cc4c02",
"#993404",
"#662506"
]

import holoviews as hv
hv.extension('bokeh')

import geopandas as gpd
import hvplot.pandas
import holoviews as hv
hv.extension('bokeh')

melted_df = gpd.read_file("/Users/dennisespejo/Desktop/Projects/Final_Project/analysis/Melted_DF.geojson")


melted_df["Population"] = melted_df["Population"].fillna(0)

# (optional but recommended if other columns show in hover)
melted_df["Median Household Income"] = melted_df["Median Household Income"].fillna(0)
choropleth_map = melted_df.hvplot.polygons(
    'geometry', 
    color='Population',
    groupby='Race/Ethnicity',
    dynamic=False, 
    cmap= earth_tone_palette, 
    width=600,  
    height=600,  
    title='Choropleth of Black/White Latine Population Per Census Tract in Philadelphia',
    colorbar=True, 
    hover_cols=['tract','Median Household Income'] 
)

choropleth_map = choropleth_map.opts(
    frame_width=500, 
    show_frame=False,  
    xaxis=None,  
    yaxis=None   
)


choropleth_map

When I first came to Penn, a lot of my research was based in Afro-Latine studies. I made this map to connect the class assignment to that research interest. This app analyzes the raw population of White/Latines vs Black Latines across Philadelphia census tracts. I chose this map because its one of the first maps I’ve made on my own, and I feel it needs the most editing when reflecting on the principals and tips of good map making.

At first glance, the composition of my map already has a few structural “errors”.

First, I did the cardinal sin of displaying a choropleth map with count data, here a propotional symbol map like the one I did for my “crush” map remake would be better suited so that geographies with… (explanation for why count data doesn’t work with choropleth maps). If I wanted to keep a choropleth map and use any form of population metric, I would need to.

Second, I put the maps in a widget, although a cool trick and fun to create, if I were to transfer this into a publication, I would need a side by side map. Side by side maps are also easier to analyze differences across two or more maps.

Third, the scales for both maps aren’t the same. I initially did this because there were more White latines than Black Latines in Philadelphia, and wanted the Black Latine map to have at least some census tracts with a visible difference in populations. I question why I didnt just use the same scale from the Black/Latine map on the White/Latine map though.

The following map addresses these concerns I brought up.

Map Remake

Code 
earth_tone_palette = [
    "#ffffe5",
    "#fff7bc",
    "#fee391",
    "#fec44f",
    "#fe9929",
    "#ec7014",
    "#cc4c02",
    "#993404",
    "#662506"
]

import pandas as pd
import geopandas as gpd
import holoviews as hv
import hvplot.pandas
import geoviews.tile_sources as gvts
from shapely.geometry import Point

hv.extension("bokeh")

# -----------------------------
# Read in data
# -----------------------------
melted_df = gpd.read_file(
    "/Users/dennisespejo/Desktop/Projects/Final_Project/analysis/Melted_DF.geojson"
)

melted_df = melted_df.to_crs(epsg=3857)

# -----------------------------
# Clean numeric columns
# -----------------------------
total_pop_col = "Total Population"

melted_df["Population"] = pd.to_numeric(
    melted_df["Population"], errors="coerce"
)

melted_df[total_pop_col] = (
    melted_df[total_pop_col]
    .astype(str)
    .str.replace(",", "", regex=False)
)
melted_df[total_pop_col] = pd.to_numeric(
    melted_df[total_pop_col], errors="coerce"
)

melted_df["Median Household Income"] = pd.to_numeric(
    melted_df["Median Household Income"], errors="coerce"
)

melted_df = melted_df[~melted_df.geometry.is_empty]
melted_df = melted_df[melted_df.geometry.is_valid]

# -----------------------------
# Keep only groups of interest
# -----------------------------
groups_of_interest = [
    "Black and Latino/Hispanic",
    "White and Latino/Hispanic"
]

melted_df = melted_df[
    melted_df["Race/Ethnicity"].isin(groups_of_interest)
].copy()

# -----------------------------
# Build full tract x group grid
# -----------------------------
tract_base = (
    melted_df[["tract", "geometry", total_pop_col, "Median Household Income"]]
    .drop_duplicates(subset=["tract"])
    .copy()
)

tract_base[total_pop_col] = tract_base[total_pop_col].fillna(0)
tract_base["Median Household Income"] = tract_base["Median Household Income"].fillna(0)

full_index = pd.MultiIndex.from_product(
    [tract_base["tract"].unique(), groups_of_interest],
    names=["tract", "Race/Ethnicity"]
)

full_grid = pd.DataFrame(index=full_index).reset_index()

full_grid = full_grid.merge(tract_base, on="tract", how="left")

full_grid = full_grid.merge(
    melted_df[["tract", "Race/Ethnicity", "Population"]],
    on=["tract", "Race/Ethnicity"],
    how="left"
)

full_grid["Population"] = full_grid["Population"].fillna(0)

map_df = gpd.GeoDataFrame(full_grid, geometry="geometry", crs=melted_df.crs)

# -----------------------------
# Percent of tract
# -----------------------------
map_df["pct_of_tract"] = 0
mask = map_df[total_pop_col] > 0
map_df.loc[mask, "pct_of_tract"] = (
    map_df.loc[mask, "Population"] / map_df.loc[mask, total_pop_col]
) * 100

map_df["pct_of_tract"] = map_df["pct_of_tract"].fillna(0)

# -----------------------------
# Split groups
# -----------------------------
black_latine = map_df[
    map_df["Race/Ethnicity"] == "Black and Latino/Hispanic"
].copy()

white_latine = map_df[
    map_df["Race/Ethnicity"] == "White and Latino/Hispanic"
].copy()

shared_max = map_df["pct_of_tract"].max()

main_title = (
    "Black Latino residents concentrate in North Philly; "
    "White Latino residents extend into the Northeast"
)

# -----------------------------
# Bounds
# -----------------------------
philly_union = map_df.geometry.unary_union
xmin, ymin, xmax, ymax = philly_union.bounds

xpad = (xmax - xmin) * 0.02
ypad = (ymax - ymin) * 0.02

xmin, xmax = xmin - xpad, xmax + xpad
ymin, ymax = ymin - ypad, ymax + ypad

# -----------------------------
# Faint basemap
# -----------------------------
tiles = gvts.CartoLight.opts(
    width=430,
    height=500,
    alpha=0.2,
    xaxis=None,
    yaxis=None,
    tools=["pan", "wheel_zoom", "reset"]
)

# -----------------------------
# Custom labels
# -----------------------------
labels_df = pd.DataFrame({
    "name": [
        "North Philly", "Northeast", "West Philly",
        "Center City", "South Philly"
    ],
    "lon": [-75.158, -75.060, -75.225, -75.165, -75.170],
    "lat": [40.005, 40.055, 39.965, 39.952, 39.925]
})

labels_gdf = gpd.GeoDataFrame(
    labels_df,
    geometry=[Point(xy) for xy in zip(labels_df.lon, labels_df.lat)],
    crs="EPSG:4326"
).to_crs(epsg=3857)

labels_gdf["x"] = labels_gdf.geometry.x
labels_gdf["y"] = labels_gdf.geometry.y

labels_bg = hv.Labels(labels_gdf, ["x", "y"], "name").opts(
    text_font_size="11pt",
    text_color="white"
)

labels_fg = hv.Labels(labels_gdf, ["x", "y"], "name").opts(
    text_font_size="10pt",
    text_color="black"
)

label_layer = labels_bg * labels_fg

# -----------------------------
# Polygons
# -----------------------------
black_polygons = black_latine.hvplot.polygons(
    "geometry",
    c="pct_of_tract",
    cmap=earth_tone_palette,
    clim=(0, shared_max),
    colorbar=False,
    alpha=0.82,
    line_color="white",
    line_width=0.5,
).opts(width=430, height=500, xlim=(xmin, xmax), ylim=(ymin, ymax))

white_polygons = white_latine.hvplot.polygons(
    "geometry",
    c="pct_of_tract",
    cmap=earth_tone_palette,
    clim=(0, shared_max),
    colorbar=True,
    clabel="Percent of Tract Population",
    alpha=0.82,
    line_color="white",
    line_width=0.5,
).opts(
    width=430,
    height=500,
    colorbar_position="bottom",
    xlim=(xmin, xmax),
    ylim=(ymin, ymax)
)

# -----------------------------
# Overlay
# -----------------------------
black_map = (tiles * black_polygons * label_layer).opts(
    title="Black Latino/Hispanic",
    fontsize={"title": 11}
)

white_map = (tiles * white_polygons * label_layer).opts(
    title="White Latino/Hispanic",
    fontsize={"title": 11}
)

# -----------------------------
# Final layout
# -----------------------------
side_by_side_map = (black_map + white_map).cols(2).opts(
    title=main_title,
    fontsize={"title": 13},
    toolbar="right"
)

side_by_side_map

I remade this map with some AI assistance, and prompting it with my suggested edits based on what I wrote above.

I fixed the scale by adding it based on percentage of White/Latine or Black/Latine across the total tract, thus making it more in line with the necessary data needed for a choropleth map.

I then put the maps side by side to facilitate comparison across both maps, and made sure that both maps have the same color scale/legend so that data is clear and not misrepresnted. As in, a darker tract on my left map is around the equivalent amount of a darker shaded tract on my right map. I added actual neighborhoods over the map, so person’s can identify which areas of the city the heavier concentrated areas of White/Latine and Black/Latine residents are located in. Next, I added a base map to contextualize Philadelphia with its surrounding area and it doesn’t look like a floating island, which I feel helps ground my map as a more realtistic and tangible finding.

As we learned in class, I added a main takeaway to my map, so that even if my map is covered up, a reader will be able to tell what Im attempting to portray with my map before examining it.

espejo@upenn.edu
